english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authori
ugf_response: The first idea is a rule that says a group that runs a place is called a top power when and only when it has the highest power to make rules for that place. In other words the rule says that having the highest power is both a must and enough for a group to be called a top power. The second idea is an example that is meant to show the rule does not work. The example says that there is a group that has the highest power to make rules for a place but people do not call that group a top power, so the person thinks the rule is wrong. The thinking that a single example that does not fit the rule can show the rule is false is a good way to test a rule that says “if and only if”, because one case where the rule fails breaks the claim that the two things always go together. However the quality of the thinking also depends on whether the example really has the highest power in the way the rule means. If the group in the example does not actually have the highest power, or if the rule also needs other things besides the highest power, then the example does not prove the rule false. So the reasoning is good if the example truly meets the condition the rule talks about, and not good if the example misses part of what the rule requires.

english_query: how to decide whose good matters and how much
ugf_response: First, we think about the people who get some good from a thing or an act. Then we ask what kind of good it is and if it makes a person feel happy, safe, or able to get what they need. Next, we look at how big that good is, thinking of a small good like a kind word and a big good like a new home or a chance to get food. Then we compare the good for different people and ask if one person’s good is more or less than another’s, and we also think about if the good for one person might take away something from another. After we have looked at who gets the good and how big it is, we can decide which good we want to think about more, maybe the good that helps the most people, or the good that helps the people who need it most, or the good that makes the world feel more fair. In the end the decision comes from a feeling and a thought that we make by looking at who is helped, how much they are helped, and how it fits with what we think is a fair way to share good. This step by step way lets anyone who has not studied this before see how we can pick whose good matters and how much it matters.

english_query: anomaly analogy (predictive) — cross_jurisdictional
ugf_response:
The first idea the speaker says is that there is an odd thing that has happened, and that odd thing looks a lot like another odd thing that happened before. The speaker points out that the two odd things share many of the same parts, so the claim that the two are alike is true in the sense that the facts they give match.

The second idea the speaker draws from that claim is that because the two odd things are alike, we can make a guess about what will happen when the same kind of odd thing shows up in other places that have their own rules. The speaker says that the match between the first odd thing and the second odd thing means we should expect the same kind of result in those other places, and that this expectation will guide what people do there.

When we look at whether the thinking is good, we see that the first part – noticing that the two odd things share many parts – is a solid observation. The step from that observation to the guess about other places, however, depends on whether the two odd things are truly the same in the ways that matter for the other places. If the two odd things only look alike on the surface but differ in deeper parts that affect the rules of the other places, then the guess may not be reliable. So the thinking works well when the similarity is deep and the rules of the other places are similar, but it can be weak when the similarity is only shallow or the other places have different rules. In short, the claim that the two odd things are alike is true, the claim that this means the same result will happen elsewhere follows only when the deeper parts match, and the overall reasoning is good only if those deeper parts do match.

ugf_response:
The first idea is a claim about a group of numbers that can be put in a line. It says that such a group has no missing spots if and only if you can always find a whole number that is bigger than any piece of the group. In other words, the claim says the two ways of looking at the group are exactly the same.

The second idea is what the person says they have found. They say they have a case of a group of numbers that can be put in a line and that seems to have no missing spots, but for which you cannot always find a whole number bigger than any piece. They think that this case shows the claim in the first idea cannot be right, because it gives a situation where one side of the claim is true while the other side is not.

To see whether the thinking is good we have to check the case. First we must be sure that the case really does have no missing spots. If it does, then we look at whether you can always find a whole number bigger than any piece. If the case fails this second part, then the case truly shows the two sides are not always the same and the claim in the first idea is false, so the thinking would be good. If, however, the case actually does allow you to find such a whole number, or if it actually has a missing spot, then the case does not break the claim and the person’s step from the first idea to the second idea is not correct. In that situation the thinking would be weak because the example does not really do what it was said to do. Thus the quality of the reasoning depends on whether the case truly has the features the person says it has. If it does, the reasoning is solid; if it does not, the reasoning is mistaken.

english_query: why we should not accept something just because many people believe it
ugf_response:
Person 1: Why should we not just go with what lots of people think?  
Person 2: Because what lots of people think can be wrong.

Person 1: But if many think it, isn’t it safe?  
Person 2: Not always. A group can be led by a mistake and still be big.  
Person 1: So we should look at the facts ourselves?  
Person 2: Yes, we should check the facts and think for ourselves.

english_query: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
ugf_response: First, we see a person say a thing. Then we notice that the thing can be true or not true just like any other thing. The way the person acts, whether they are kind or mean, does not make the thing become true. This is because whether a thing is true depends on how it fits with what we can see or test, not on who says it. When we look at the thing we can check if it works in the world. If it works, we can call it true, no matter who said it. If it does not work, it is not true, even if the person is liked or has a good way of acting. So we must keep the thing and the person different. This means that a good or bad way a person acts does not pick if the thing is real.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in t
ugf_response:
The first idea is a claim about what makes a word have what it is about. It says that a word has what it is about just when the word brings a picture in the mind. In other words, the claim says that the only way a word can be about something is if it makes a picture appear inside a person’s mind, and that any word that makes such a picture does have what it is about.

The second idea is a case that the person puts forward to show that the claim is not always right. They talk about a word that many people use and that clearly has what it is about, but that most people do not see a picture in their mind when they hear it. They also give a second case of a sound that can make a picture appear in a mind even though it is not a word that people use to talk about anything. Both of these cases are said to be true: the first case really does have a word that works without a picture, and the second case really does have a sound that makes a picture without being a word that has what it is about.

The third idea is what the person thinks follows from those two true cases. They say that because there is at least one word that works without a picture and at least one thing that makes a picture without being a word, the claim that a word has what it is about exactly when it brings a picture in the mind must be false. In short, they think the claim is wrong because the two cases break it.

The fourth idea is an evaluation of the thinking. The step from the true cases to saying the claim is false is a good step, because showing even one exception is enough to make a claim that says “always” turn out to be wrong. The thinking is also clear: the person first shows the two cases, then explains why those cases do not fit the claim, and finally says the claim does not hold. The only possible weak point would be if the claim were meant to be about a special kind of word or a special kind of picture, but the person does not say that. As it stands, the reasoning is solid and the conclusion that the original claim is not correct follows well from the two true cases.

english_query: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n can
ugf_response:

The case says that those two things are exactly the same as what we call a whole number that has no other pieces that multiply together to make it. In other words, the first idea is that the two simple rules are meant to match the whole‑number idea that has only the pieces one and itself.

The second idea is that, because the two simple rules match the whole‑number idea, any whole number that follows the two rules must be one of those special whole numbers, and any of those special whole numbers must follow the two rules. The case then brings up a particular whole number as a test. It says that this particular whole number follows the two simple rules but is not one of the special whole numbers, or that it is one of the special whole numbers but does not follow the two simple rules. This is meant to show that the claim that the two rules and the special whole‑number idea are exactly the same is not correct.

The thinking that follows from the second idea is that, if the test whole number really does what the case says, then the claim that the two rules and the special whole‑number idea are exactly the same must be false. That step is only good if the test whole number truly meets the description given. If the test whole number does not actually meet both of the simple rules, or if it is actually one of the special whole numbers, then the step does not work and the case does not break the claim. In that situation the thinking would be weak because it would be using a wrong example. If, however, the test whole number does meet the two rules and still is not one of the special whole numbers, then the thinking is strong and the original claim would indeed be shown to be false.

english_query: material conditional, affirming_consequent — testimonial
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a claim that there is a link between two things: if the first thing is true then the second thing will happen. They say that this link exists as a rule that can be true or false, just like any other claim about how things work. The second idea they take from that claim is a step that says, because we see the second thing happening, the first thing must also be true. In other words, they turn the rule around and think that the presence of the result tells us that the cause must have been there. The third idea they add is that a person has said the second thing is true, and they use that person’s words as proof that the first thing is true as well. They treat the person’s statement as a kind of evidence that the whole claim is right.

Looking at the first idea alone, the rule “if the first thing is true then the second thing will happen” can be true if it matches how the world works, but it can also be false if it does not match. So the first idea is not automatically true; it needs its own check. The second idea, the step that moves from seeing the second thing to saying the first thing must be true, does not follow from the rule. The rule only says that the first thing can lead to the second, not that the second can only come from the first. This step is therefore a weak way to think and does not give a reliable conclusion. The third idea, using a person’s words about the second thing as proof for the first thing, adds another weak step. Even if the person is right about the second thing, that does not fix the problem that the jump from the second thing back to the first thing is not a good way to think.

Putting the pieces together, the claim that the first thing is true because a person said the second thing is true and because the second thing happened does not hold up. The first part may be true or false on its own, but the way they link the parts is not a good way to think. In short, the thinking moves from a rule to a reverse claim and then leans on a person’s words, and each of those moves is not a strong path to a correct conclusion. The overall reasoning is therefore not reliable.

english_query: the problem with thinking you know more than you really do
ugf_response:
Person 1: I sometimes feel like I know everything about a subject. Why can that be a problem?  
Person 2: When you think you know more than you really do, you may stop listening to other people and miss new or better ideas.  
Person 1: So it can keep me from learning?  
Person 2: Yes, because you may think you already have the answer and not look for evidence that could change your view.  
Person 1: Does it also affect how I work with other people?  
Person 2: It does. Others may feel you do not respect what they say, and they might not want to share what they know, which makes teamwork harder.  
Person 1: How can I notice when I am doing this?  
Person 2: Try to ask yourself if you are ready to hear a different point, and check if you can find proof for what you think before you act.  
Person 1: What should I do if I find out I was wrong?  
Person 2: Admit it, thank the person who helped you see it, and use the new info to make a better choice next time.

english_query: the idea that you should only follow rules everyone could agree to
ugf_response: First, when a person does something, the rest of the people can see it and then they might choose to do the same thing or do something different. This shows that one act can make many people think about what they should do. If we want a way that all people can feel is right, we look for a thing that most people can say yes to and then we all do that thing. In other words, we try to find a way that everyone could agree on and then each person follows that way. When many people follow the same agreed way, it helps keep things fair and safe for the group. If someone does something that most people do not like, the others may feel hurt or angry and the group may not stay together. So the idea is that we should try to pick a way that all people can say yes to and then each person should follow that way, because it makes the group work better for everyone.

english_query: anomaly analogy (argumentative) — epistemology
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim about a thing that does not fit the usual pattern. The person says that this thing really does happen and that we can see it in the facts that are there. They are saying that the fact is true because we have a clear example that does not match what we normally expect.

The second idea is the thought that because we have this odd thing, we can compare it to another situation and say that the same kind of rule must work there too. The person thinks that the odd thing shows that the rule we use in one area can be used in the other area, and they say that this follows from the first idea. In other words, they think that the odd thing lets us make a comparison that tells us how things work in a different place.

The third part is a check of whether the thought that follows is right. To see if the thinking is good, we need to ask if the comparison really shows the same kind of rule or if the odd thing is just a special case that does not tell us about the other place. If the two places are not alike in the important ways, then the thought that the rule works in both places is not right. If they are alike, then the thought may be right. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the two situations match. If they match well, the thinking is good; if they do not, the thinking is not good.

english_query: counterexample to: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts i
ugf_response:

In other words, they say that the only way to know if someone is brave is to see them do that kind of act again and again, and that anyone who does that kind of act again and again must be brave.

The second idea they bring in is a case that shows the rule does not work. They point to a person who often does the kind of act the rule talks about, but who does not seem to have the good quality of being brave, or they point to a person who has the good quality of being brave but who does not often do the act. The third idea they think follows from that case is that the rule itself must be wrong, or at least must be changed. That thinking is a good step, because showing even one case where a rule does not fit is enough to show the rule is not always true. However, if they go on to say that the rule is never true for anyone, that would be too strong, because the rule might still be true for many people even if it is not true for every single person. So the move from the case to the idea that the rule is not always true is solid, while the move to the idea that the rule is never true is not fully supported.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A
ugf_response: The first idea that the person says is that a claim that a talk trying to show a god exists is good just when the talk itself is true. The second idea the person thinks follows from that is that because they have found a case where the talk is true and yet not good, the original claim that the two always go together must be wrong. The third part is to check whether that thinking is good. If the case really does have a talk that is true and also not good, then it does show that the original claim does not hold in all cases, so the thinking that the original claim is false is sound. If, however, the case does not truly have a talk that is both true and not good, then the step from the case to saying the original claim is false would not be a good step. In short, the person’s argument works only if the example they give truly meets the two parts of the original claim; if it does, their conclusion that the original claim is wrong is a good one.

english_query: how to think about what the other person will do before you act
ugf_response: First, you think about what the other person may do. You can do this by looking at what they have done before and by hearing what they say. You can also see how they move and how they act. When you have a thought, you can make a move about what you will do. If you think the other will go one way, you may go another way. If you think they will stay, you may go. You can also talk with them, but if you cannot talk, you use what you have seen. You keep looking as you go, because the other can change what they do. By doing this step by step, you can act in a way that works with what the other person will do.

english_query: literal_similarity analogy (explanatory) — mechanical_and_repair
ugf_response: The first idea they say is that a bad part of a machine and a bad part of a car look the same in a very clear way. They point out that the shape and the look of the two parts match, so they claim that this match is a fact that can be trusted. The second idea they think follows from that fact is that because the two parts look the same, the way to fix them must also be the same. They use the picture of the two parts to explain why the same tool or the same steps that work on the machine part should work on the car part as well. The third part of the case is the judgment about whether this line of thought is good. The thinking is not very strong because just because two things look the same does not always mean they work the same or need the same fix. Different machines can have hidden parts or different inner ways that are not seen on the outside, so a method that works on one may not work on the other. In this sense the first idea may be true about the look, but the step that the second idea makes from that look to the same fix is a weak step. The overall thinking, therefore, is only partly right: it shows a real similarity in look, but it jumps too quickly to a claim about how to act, and that jump is not well supported.

english_query: how two things happening together does not mean one causes the other
ugf_response: First, we notice that sometimes we see two events at the same time. Then we might think that one of them makes the other happen. This idea can be wrong because the two events can both be the result of something else, or they can just happen by chance. To see if one really makes the other happen, we would need to watch what happens when we change one of them while keeping everything else the same. If the other one still happens the same way, then the first one is not the reason. If the other one changes when we change the first one, then the first one may be the reason. So just because two things happen together does not mean one makes the other happen.

english_query: the difference between a strong reason and a weak reason
ugf_response:
When people talk about why they do something, they give a reason. One reason can feel solid and can make many people feel it is a good cause to act, while another reason can feel shaky and can leave people unsure whether to act. The scene we look at is a person who says they will do something because they have a reason, and other people hear that reason and have to decide if it is strong enough to make them do the same thing or something else.

The hard question that comes up is how we can tell if a reason is strong or weak. Some people say a strong reason is one that fits with what we can see and check, that lines up with what has happened before, and that would lead to good results for the people involved. Others say a strong reason is one that feels right inside, that matches the ideas we hold dear, or that makes us feel safe and calm. Still other people think a weak reason is one that comes from only a little bit of information, that is based on a feeling that can change quickly, or that does not show a clear link to the result we want.

Different answers appear because people look at the same reason in different ways. One group of people will look for proof that the reason matches real facts, so they call it strong when they can see a clear connection between the cause and the effect. Another group will look at how the reason fits with their own ideas about what is right or what feels fair, so they call it strong when it matches those ideas, even if they cannot point to clear proof. A third group may say a reason is weak when it seems to come only from a quick feeling or from a wish that something will happen, because they think a reason needs more solid proof before they will act on it. Each of these views makes sense to the people who hold them, and the mix of these views shows why it can be hard to agree on where the line between a strong reason and a weak reason should be.

english_query: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a fun
ugf_response:
The first idea says that two groups have the same number of things if and only if there is a way that puts each thing in the first group together with exactly one different thing in the second group, and also puts each thing in the second group together with exactly one different thing in the first group. In other words, a perfect back and forth matching must exist for the two groups to have the same amount. 

The person then gives a situation to show that this idea is not always true. They look at a first group that has three numbers: one, two and three. They look at a second group that has four letters: a, b, c and d. They point out a way that puts each number together with a different letter: one with a, two with b, three with c. This way each number gets a different letter, but the letter d is left without a number. So there is a way that puts each thing in the first group with a different thing in the second group, but not a way that does the same for every thing in the second group. The two groups do not have the same number of things because the second group has one more thing than the first. 

From this situation the person thinks that the second idea follows: because we can find a way that matches every thing in the first group with a different thing in the second group while the groups still have different amounts, the original claim that such a way is enough for the groups to have the same amount must be false. 

The thinking is good if the original claim was indeed that only one‑way matching is needed for the groups to have the same amount. The example correctly shows a case where a one‑way matching exists but the numbers of things are not equal, so it shows that the original claim would be wrong in that form. If, however, the original claim meant that a two‑way matching must exist, then the example does not affect it, because the example does not give a two‑way matching. In that case the person’s jump from the example to saying the whole claim is false would not be a good step. Overall, the person’s description of the situation is true, and the step that the situation makes the first idea false is a good step only when the first idea was stated in the weaker way that the person is trying to reject.

english_query: anomaly analogy (argumentative) — philosophy_of_mind
ugf_response:
First, the speaker says that there is an odd thing that does not fit with the usual way we see thoughts and feelings work. The speaker says that this odd thing really does happen and that it looks different from the normal pattern we see in most people. That claim can be checked by looking at the odd thing and seeing if it is truly out of the ordinary, and in many cases it does appear to be out of the ordinary, so the first idea is at least something we can accept as true.

Second, the speaker says that because this odd thing is out of the ordinary, it must show that thoughts can exist apart from the body that holds them. The speaker moves from the fact that the odd thing is different to the thought that the mind can be separate from the body. That is the second idea that the speaker thinks follows from the first.

Third, we look at whether the move from the first idea to the second idea makes sense. The step from an odd thing to the claim that thoughts can be separate needs a clear link, and without more proof it can be a weak step. It could be that the odd thing has other reasons that do not require a separate mind, so the thinking may not be very good. If the odd thing shares the same important parts that we see in normal thought, then the move might be stronger, but without that extra check the thinking is on shaky ground. In short, the first claim can be true, the second claim follows only if we accept a strong link, and the link as given is not very strong, so the overall thinking is not very good.

english_query: what makes a good reason for believing something
ugf_response:
Person 1: What makes a good reason for believing something?  
Person 2: A good reason is something that fits with what we already see and can be checked.  
Person 1: How do we know if it fits with what we see?

Person 1: What if we cannot see it directly?  
Person 2: Then we look for other things that point to it, like other people who have checked it.  
Person 1: How do we know those other people are not wrong?  
Person 2: We see if many different people get the same result each time.  
Person 1: So a good reason is when many people see the same thing and it matches what we know?  
Person 2: Yes, that is a strong sign that it may be true.

It says that W is a whole that is made of a group of pieces we call P1, P2, …, Pn, and that this is true only when each piece has a certain feature that lets it belong to the whole. The second idea is an example that tries to show the statement does not always work. In the example there is a thing that is made of pieces, but one of the pieces does not have the feature the statement says every piece must have, yet the thing still works as a whole. This seems to go against the statement. The thinking behind the example is that if we can find even one case where the rule does not hold, then the rule must be wrong. That move can be good when the rule says it must hold for all cases. However, the rule might have extra ideas that were not written down, such as a need for each piece to be able to connect in a certain way. english_query: indicative conditional, modus_ponens — consequentialist
ugf_response:
The first idea the person says is a claim that looks like this: if one thing happens, then another thing will happen. In other words, they say there is a link between a first event and a later event. Whether that claim is true depends on whether the link they point to really exists in the world. If the link is real, then the claim can be true; if the link is not real, the claim is false.

The second idea the person uses is a way of moving from the first claim to a new claim. They say that because the first event has in fact happened, we can be sure that the later event will also happen. This is a common way of thinking that many people use: when we have a claim that says “if A then B” and we see that A is true, we then say B must be true. This step follows the rule correctly, so if the first claim is true, the step to the new claim is a good step.

The third idea the person adds is a view about why we should care about the first claim. They say that we should look at the later event – the result – to decide whether the first event was a good thing to do. In plain words, they are saying that what matters most is what happens after the first event. This view can be helpful because it makes us think about the real outcomes of actions, but it can also be a problem if we ignore other things like the reasons for the action or how people feel about it. So the thinking that the result is the only thing that matters is not always a safe way to judge, even though it can give useful guidance in many cases.

english_query: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that they have an example where a line of words from one talk is changed into another talk but the change does not keep the same meaning, and yet the rule that says a change is good enough only when the meaning stays the same would say this change should not be called good enough. They point to this one case to show that the rule does not work for every line of words.

The second idea they take from that is that because they can find even one line of words that makes the rule fail, the rule itself must be false. They think that the existence of this one case is enough to say the rule cannot be true in general.

The third idea is an evaluation of whether that step from the first idea to the second idea is a good step. If the rule was meant to apply to all possible lines of words without any extra limits, then showing a single line that does not fit does indeed show the rule is wrong, and the thinking is good. But if the rule was meant to apply only when certain other conditions are also met, such as when the people using the two talks share the same background or when the line is short, then the example might not meet those extra conditions and the rule might still hold in the cases it was meant for. In that situation the step from the example to saying the whole rule is false would be too quick, and the thinking would not be fully good. So whether the reasoning is good depends on how the rule was originally meant to be used.

english_query: how to find the hidden parts of someone's case
ugf_response: First, we look at what the person says and does in the open. Then we ask them to tell more about why they did each thing and what they felt inside. Next, we watch how they act when no one is looking so we can see the parts that are not shown at first. After that we ask other people who know the person to tell us what they have seen and heard. We also look at any paper or note the person has left that might give signs. By putting together what we see, what they tell us, what others tell us, and what the paper says, we can bring out the parts of the person's story that were not clear before. In this way we find the parts that were not in plain view.

english_query: the problem of hurting one person to help many
ugf_response: First, we see that when we think about doing something that will hurt one person we might think it will help many other people. Then we note that hurting a person means that that person feels pain and a bad feeling. This can make the rest of the people feel uneasy because they see a person being hurt. Next, we think about why someone might say it is okay to hurt one for many. They may say that the good for many is bigger than the hurt for one. But we also see that the true hurt is not just a number; it is a real feeling for a real person. Then we put side by side the true hurt of one with the maybe good for many and see that we do not have a simple way to do that. Finally, we understand that because of this problem many people think we should look for ways that do not need to hurt anyone, or we should try to find other ways that can help many without causing hurt. This shows why the idea of hurting one to help many is hard to accept.

english_query: material conditional, paradoxical — evidential
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a simple rule that says if one thing is true then another thing is true. In other words, the speaker says that this kind of rule can be true in the way that a true statement about a fact can be true. The second idea is that this rule can lead to a situation that looks like a problem that does not fit the normal way we think about things. The third idea is that the speaker thinks we can see this from what we know about the world, that the picture we have from what we see is enough to say the whole thing is true.  

From these three ideas the speaker then says that because the first rule is true, the strange looking problem must also be true, and because we can see it, we have enough reason to accept it. The speaker also says that this shows how we can use the rule to understand other things that seem odd.  

When we look at the thinking, the first idea is fine because a rule of the form if‑then can be true in many cases. The step that says the rule must make a problem that does not fit, however, is not solid. A simple if‑then rule does not by itself create a problem that seems to go against what we expect, so the link between the rule and the strange looking problem is weak. The move from what we see to saying the whole thing is true also needs more support, because what we see may not tell the whole story. For these reasons the overall thinking is only partly good: the starting rule is true, but the jump to the odd looking problem and the claim that what we see is enough to prove it are not fully justified.

english_query: counterexample to: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward says that a group A is bigger than a group B exactly when A has more things than B. For groups that we can count one by one this sounds right, but the claim is not true for groups that go on forever. To show this, the speaker gives a case that looks at the group of all whole numbers and the group of all even whole numbers. Even though every even whole number is also a whole number, we can line up each whole number with an even whole number by pairing each whole number n with the even number 2 × n. Because we can make this one‑to‑one pairing, the two groups have the same size, even though one group seems to have fewer things. So the fact that this case works is true, and it shows that the first idea is not true in general.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because the first idea does not hold, we cannot decide which group is bigger just by looking at how many things each group seems to have. The speaker thinks that the case they gave proves that the way of saying “more things means bigger” must be changed. This step of reasoning is good in the sense that the example does indeed break the old rule, so it is reasonable to look for a different rule. However, if the speaker then says that no rule at all can ever tell us which of two groups is bigger, that would be a bad step because there are other ways, such as the one‑to‑one pairing idea, that do work. In short, the factual claim about the two groups of numbers is true, the inference that the old “more things means bigger” rule fails is a correct move, and the quality of the thinking is good as long as it stops at pointing out the need for a new rule rather than claiming that no comparison is possible.

english_query: indicative conditional, modus_tollens — criminal
ugf_response:
First, the case says that there is a way that says if a person does a bad thing that breaks the law then we can call that person a law breaker. It also says that if we see that a person is not a law breaker, then we can say that the person did not do the bad thing. The first thought of the case is that this first way is true.

Second, the case thinks that because we can see that a person is not a law breaker, we are allowed to say that the person did not do the bad thing. In other words, the second thought is that the first way lets us move from the fact that the person is not a law breaker to the fact that the person did not do the bad thing.

Third, we look at whether this thinking is good. If the first way is always true, then the step that the case takes is okay. But the first way is not always true, because a person can do a bad thing that breaks the law and still not be called a law breaker right away, or a person can be called a law breaker for a reason that is not the bad thing we are looking at. Because the first way can be wrong sometimes, the step that the case makes can lead to a wrong idea. So the thinking is only as good as the first way, and the first way is not something we can trust all the time. This means the case’s reasoning can be weak.

english_query: anomaly analogy (heuristic) — combinatorial
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being said as true is that when we see a single odd thing we can use it like a picture to think about many different ways that things can be put together. In other words, the one strange example is treated as a stand‑in for a whole set of possible examples, and the claim is that this stand‑in really does show something about the whole set.

The second idea is that, because the picture from the odd thing seems to work, we can then say that it also works for the other things we have not yet seen. So the thought moves from the one example to a larger group, and the claim is that the rule we make from the first example will give us a good guess for the many other cases that could happen.

The third idea looks at whether that move from one example to many is a good move. It is good in the sense that it can let us act quickly and see patterns without having to check every single case, but it can be a bad move if the first odd thing is not like the rest. When the first thing is very different, the picture we made may lead us to a wrong guess, so the thinking works only when the first example is a fair stand‑in for the others. In short, the claim can be useful, but it must be checked that the first odd thing really does share the important features of the larger group before we let the idea guide our next steps.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denia
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward is a claim about when a statement must be true in every possible situation. It says that a statement will be true in all cases exactly when two things happen: first, the statement is true right now, and second, the meaning of the statement works out as true in every possible case. The person who makes the claim thinks that these two points are enough to guarantee that the statement is always true.

The second idea is an example that is meant to show that the claim does not work. In this example there is a statement that is indeed true right now and whose meaning also looks true in every possible case, but when we look more closely we see a situation where the statement fails. This shows that having the two conditions does not always make the statement true in every possible case. The example therefore tries to show that the first claim is not correct.

The third idea is the reasoning that goes from the example to the judgment that the original claim is false. The reasoning says that because we can find a case where the two conditions are met and the statement still is not true in every possible case, the claim that the two conditions are enough must be wrong. This line of thinking is good if the example really does meet the two conditions and still fails to be always true. If the example does meet those conditions, then the reasoning correctly shows a problem with the claim. If, however, the example does not truly meet one of the two conditions, then the reasoning would be based on a mistake. Assuming the example is correct, the thinking is solid and shows that the original claim needs to be changed or added to. If the example is not correct, then the thinking would not be good because it would be based on a false picture. In short, the truth of the claim depends on whether the example truly satisfies the two stated points, and the logic that moves from the example to the judgment is only as strong as that verification.

english_query: anomaly analogy (heuristic) — cross_jurisdictional
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is that when we see a strange event in one place, we can look at it and think it shows something that might also happen in another place that follows a different set of rules. They say this is true because the odd event shows a break in the normal pattern and that break can be used as a clue for other places that have similar parts but different overall rules.

The second idea they think follows from that is that we can then use the clue from the first place to guess how the other places will act. They think that because the odd event gave us a clear sign, we can make a quick way to think about other places and decide what to do there. In other words, they think the clue lets us make a short plan for many places without having to see the same odd event in each one.

The third idea is about whether that short plan is a good way to think. The thinking can be good if the odd event really does show a part that works the same in all places, because then the short plan will match what actually happens. But the thinking can be bad if the odd event only fits the first place and does not match the other places, because then the short plan will miss important differences. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the odd event really shows a part that is the same in many places, and on how careful we are to check that the other places do not have other parts that change the result.

english_query: how the way choices are shown can change what you pick
ugf_response: First, we see that when many things to pick are put before us, we first look at the ones that stand out or that we see first. Then we see that those first things get more of our look, so we may maybe pick one of them. Next, we think that if the things are put in a place, the ones at the first part of the place feel easier to reach, so we may pick one of those. This means that the way the things are shown can push us toward a certain pick. Then we see that if the things are shown with pictures or with words that make them look good, we may feel that they are better and we pick them. This shows that the form of the show can change what we end up picking. Finally, we understand that by changing the look of the show we can change many people picks, even if the things themselves do not change.

english_query: why saying something over and over does not make it true
ugf_response:
Person 1: Why do some people think that saying something a lot makes it right?  
Person 2: Because they feel that hearing it many times makes their mind accept it.  
Person 1: But does hearing it many times change if it matches the facts?  
Person 2: No, the facts stay the same whether we hear them many times or not.  
Person 1: So why do we sometimes believe an idea just because we hear it a lot?  
Person 2: Our brain likes things that feel familiar, and it can think familiar things are safe.  
Person 1: Can we check if an idea is right even if we have heard it many times?  
Person 2: Yes, we can look at real proof or see if it fits with what we can see and test.  
Person 1: What should we do when we hear an idea again and again that seems wrong?  
Person 2: We should stop and ask for proof, and not let the number of times we hear it decide for us.

english_query: why sometimes a good enough answer is better than the best answer
ugf_response: When we look at a problem we can try to find a perfect answer that fixes everything the way we want. That perfect answer can be hard to find, can need a lot of time, and can need a lot of work that may not be ready when we need to act. At the same time we can find an answer that is not perfect but that works well enough for the moment. It may not solve every tiny part of the problem, but it can be ready quickly and can let us move forward. The hard question in this situation is whether we should wait for the perfect answer or use the good enough answer that we already have. Some people say that we should always wait for the perfect answer because any mistake can grow later and cost more. They think that a perfect answer gives us the safest path and that a small mistake now can become a big problem later. Others say that waiting for perfect can cost us time, money, or even the chance to help people now. They argue that a good enough answer lets us act, learn, and change the answer later if we need to. Some people also point out that in a fast changing world the perfect answer may not stay perfect for long, so using a good enough answer and then fixing it later can be the smarter way. Still other people say that the choice depends on how important the problem is. If the problem could hurt many people, they may feel that we must wait for the best answer. If the problem is small or the risk is low, they may feel that a good enough answer is the right choice. In the end the situation makes us think about the trade off between speed and safety, between using what we have now and waiting for what might be better later, and about how we judge when a good enough answer is truly enough.

english_query: how we build a picture of the world from small pieces of what we see
ugf_response:
Person 1: I have a question. How do we make a picture of the world from the small parts we see?  
Person 2: When we look at one part, we keep it in our mind. Then we look at another part and add it to the first part. In this way we put many parts together until we have a whole view.  
Person 1: So we just add them one by one? What makes us put them together in the right way?  
Person 2: Our brain likes to find ways. When it sees that two parts fit, it joins them. It also uses what we already know to guess how new parts fit.  
Person 1: Does it matter what we see first?  
Person 2: Yes. The first parts can shape the shape of the whole. They act like a ground that the later parts sit on.  
Person 1: What if we miss a part?  
Person 2: Then the picture may have a hole. We may try to fill the hole with what we think should be there, or we may keep looking for the missing part.  
Person 1: How do we know when the picture is finished?  
Person 2: We feel that most of the parts we need are there and that they fit together. We can also look again at the world to see if anything does not match.

ugf_response:
The first idea is a claim that a thing can be called a real big change only when it has two parts. The claim says that the thing must have the taking down of the people who run a place and also must have another part that the claim does not name here. The person who makes the case says that they have found a thing that looks like a big change but does not have the taking down part. They tell about this thing as a way to show that the claim does not work for every case.

The second idea is what they think follows from that. They say that because they have a thing that is a big change and does not have the taking down part, the claim that both parts are needed must be wrong. In other words, they think the claim is not true because they have a case that does not fit the claim but still seems to be a big change.

The third idea is an evaluation of whether that thinking is good. If the thing they point to really is a big change and really does not have the taking down part, then their reasoning that the claim is false is strong. If, however, the thing they point to does not really count as a big change, or if it does in fact have some form of taking down that they missed, then the reasoning is weak. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the example matches what the claim says and on whether the example truly shows a big change without the first part. If both of those checks pass, the thinking is good; if not, the thinking is not good.

english_query: literal_similarity analogy (heuristic) — ethics_thought_experiments
ugf_response: The first idea says that when two things look the same in a clear way, we can take that look‑alike as a hint to think about a made‑up story that is about what is good or bad. The second idea says that because we have that hint, we can then decide in the made‑up story what is good and what is bad, and we can also let that decision help us decide about real things we see later. The thinking can be good in the sense that a simple hint often helps the mind move faster and can show a pattern that many people notice, but it can also be not so good because a simple look‑alike does not always show the deeper details that matter, so the decision we reach may be wrong if we rely only on that hint. In short, the claim that a clear similarity can be used as a clue is true, the claim that the clue then gives a full answer about good and bad follows from that claim, and the use of the clue is useful sometimes but it is not a perfect way to reach a safe answer.
